new_feedback.element: State 3 chart 'Detection 0.71' plotted against the 'recall >= 0.90' red line, while the halt banner and rationale attribute the stop to 'white-box self-test recall 0.71'
new_feedback.issue: Two different metric names (chart 'Detection' vs halt 'white-box self-test recall') share the same 0.71 value and the same red line, so an operator scanning the screen can't tell whether Detection and the gating white-box recall are the same series or two coincidentally-equal numbers. The thing that actually halted the run is ambiguous at a glance.
new_feedback.change: Make the gating series unambiguous: either rename the chart's cyan series to 'White-box recall' on the halted state so it matches the banner/rationale, or add a one-line caption under the chart stating 'Detection (live) and white-box recall (gating) are the same series' / 'are distinct — only white-box recall gates the halt'.

new_feedback.element: State 3 halting-round judge footer reads '✓ PASS · weight 1/5' while the streaming and round-22/21 rows use a bare '1/5' badge
new_feedback.issue: Reintroduces the 'weight 1/5' phrasing that iter-01 asked to be standardized to a single consistent token; the judge label is again inconsistent across states.
new_feedback.change: Drop 'weight' from the halting-round footer; use the same bare '1/5' (or '1 of 5') badge token used in every other round row so the judge weight reads identically in all states.

new_feedback.element: State 3 halt-rationale RULE block: trigger is 'recall_white_box < 0.90' but the until-clause is 'recall_held_out >= 0.90' (different variable name)
new_feedback.issue: The metric that trips the halt (recall_white_box) and the metric that lifts it (recall_held_out) are named as two different variables in the same code-styled rule, with no note explaining why recovery is measured on a different set than the trigger. An eng reading this rule literally sees a logic mismatch.
new_feedback.change: Either align the names (use recall_white_box for both, or recall_held_out for both) or add one comment line in the rule block, e.g. '// held-out, not white-box, prevents re-overfitting to the known scheme', so the deliberate asymmetry is legible rather than reading as a typo.

new_feedback.element: Cost meter SESSION column: state 1 shows '$65.58 · 7 runs · since 09:14Z'; THIS RUN ceiling is $25.00
new_feedback.issue: SESSION total ($65.58 across 7 runs) sits visually equal-weight next to the THIS RUN ceiling gate, but no session-level cap is shown, so it's unclear whether session spend is governed by anything or is purely informational. A vendor watching cost wants to know if the session figure can itself trip a stop.
new_feedback.change: Either add a session ceiling/cap next to the $65.58 (e.g. '/ $200.00 org budget') or visually demote SESSION to a muted, caption-weight figure so it reads as informational-only and only THIS RUN reads as a gated meter.
